tagged_story_for_tts:
A shifting viewpoint. Reality’s frame.

The word perspective comes from the Latin perspicere—"to see through." It’s not just what you look at, but how you look.

A cube drawn on paper is flat until shading tricks your brain into seeing depth. This is perspective: the math of angles and vanishing points that turns 2D lines into 3D illusions. Renaissance artists like Brunelleschi cracked its code in the 1400s by studying how parallel train tracks seem to meet at the horizon. Without it, paintings were stiff, like cutout dolls. With it, they became windows.

But perspective isn’t just art. A honeybee’s compound eyes split light into polarized patterns, revealing invisible flower trails. A deep-sea anglerfish’s upward-facing eyes turn the dark abyss into a starlit dome, spotting prey silhouettes against the faint glow above. Humans? Our binocular vision lets us judge distance by comparing two slightly mismatched images—try covering one eye and catching a ball.

Even time bends perspective. A child’s summer stretches endless; an adult’s decade blurs. The Andromeda Galaxy is 2.5 million light-years away, so we see it as it was when mastodons roamed Earth. Perspective is the universe’s way of saying: Where you stand changes everything.

Three related subjects are optical illusions, animal vision adaptations, and how memory warps time.
